rationale: Empirical lens, all mechanical checks green: both new tests pass verbatim; go build/vet/gofmt clean; ms-spec-grill.pre0uur.md byte-identical to `git show d1b4bebf:...` (diffed directly); ms-spec-create.pre0uur.md matches the pre-edit lifecycleSkillFiles() string; plugins/.claude/.agents mirrors byte-identical for both skills. I manually drove installSkills end-to-end: a pre-guard body refreshes to the guarded canonical; a one-word user-modified variant is left untouched with a notice (HC-6 holds). But I traced a real downstream regression: checkOpenQuestions (validate/spec.go:244) flags ANY '- [ ] ...' line in Open Questions as a hard SevError, and ApproveSpec aborts on it (confirmed by direct unit probe: the exact marker text produces severity=ERROR, message='unresolved open question: ...'). ScenarioSpecToIdle (internal/harness/scenario_spec_lifecycle.go) is a fully headless run whose prompt says 'no human available to answer questions' — the exact trigger condition — and it asserts `mindspec approve spec` SUCCEEDS (line 33). Post-fix, a headless run that follows the new guard will defer via the marker and then hard-fail approve, with no skill (ms-spec-approve/autopilot/spec-orchestrator) documenting how to react. I also live-probed the bench/grill/run_eval.sh collision risk (full eval prompt incl. Headless guard + 'this is a non-interactive batch evaluation' against fixture spec1.md, Opus): it correctly produced full multi-category findings, not a deferral — reassuring but only 1 of the eval's 20 MIN-aggregated samples.
concrete_changes_required: Reconcile the deferral marker with the Open-Questions hard-fail gate before merge. checkOpenQuestions (internal/validate/spec.go:226-248) treats any '- [ ] ...' line as a blocking SevError, and ApproveSpec (internal/approve/spec.go:47-50) aborts `mindspec approve spec` on it — I confirmed this directly by unit-probing checkOpenQuestions with the guard's exact marker text and getting severity=ERROR. internal/harness/scenario_spec_lifecycle.go's ScenarioSpecToIdle is a fully-headless scenario (prompt: 'there is NO human available to answer questions... make reasonable default decisions and proceed') that asserts `assertCommandSucceeded(t, events, "mindspec", "approve", "spec")` at line 33 — this scenario is very likely to now fail (or silently coax the agent into stripping/checking the marker itself to force approve through, defeating the audit purpose) since the marker this fix prescribes trips exactly the gate the scenario asserts passes. Either (a) update ms-spec-approve/ms-spec-autopilot/spec-orchestrator.md and the harness scenario to explicitly define and test the intended behavior when a headless-deferred marker is present at approve time (halt-and-report is the design-consistent choice, but it must be documented and the scenario updated to expect it, not a bare untested failure), or (b) route the deferral through a channel the approve gate does not hard-block on. Add a regression test (validate or approve package) that runs ValidateSpec/ApproveSpec against a spec.md whose Open Questions contains exactly '- [ ] grill deferred: headless session — run /ms-spec-grill interactively before approval.' and pins the actual intended outcome — this integration is currently completely untested.

Before merge, run bench/grill/run_eval.sh for real (MS_GRILL_RUNS>=5, live credentials) to confirm recall does not collapse toward 0 on any run. My own 1-sample spot check (same prompt construction the script uses — full guarded SKILL.md + the literal 'this is a non-interactive batch evaluation' suffix — against fixtures/spec1.md, model claude-opus-4-1) came back clean: the grill produced correctly tagged GROUNDING/SEMANTIC/SYNONYM/CONTRADICTION/EXACT_PHRASE/STRUCTURAL findings, not a deferral. That is reassuring but is only 1 of the script's 20 MIN-aggregated fixture-runs, and the CLI silently auto-remaps the script's pinned model id (a pre-existing issue, but it means the eval as currently written is not actually testing the pinned model) — a full run is cheap and removes the remaining uncertainty on a script the repo's own comments call a 'bead-blocking... invariant'.

findings.detail: Ran `go test ./internal/setup/ -run TestInstallSkills_RefreshesPreHeadlessGuard -v -count=1` verbatim: both TestInstallSkills_RefreshesPreHeadlessGuardSnapshot and ...GrillSnapshot PASS. Full `go test ./internal/setup/... -count=1` also PASS (incl. TestSkillInventory_Twelve, TestInstallSkills_LeavesUserModified). `go build ./...`, `go vet ./internal/setup/... ./internal/validate/...`, and `gofmt -l` all clean. Worktree left clean after probes (no stray files).

`git show d1b4bebf:plugins/mindspec/skills/ms-spec-grill/SKILL.md | diff - internal/setup/historical_skills/ms-spec-grill.pre0uur.md` → zero diff, byte-identical. ms-spec-create.pre0uur.md content matches the pre-edit `lifecycleSkillFiles()["ms-spec-create"]` raw string in claude.go@d1b4bebf line-for-line (manually diffed the extracted literal). Both are also proven byte-exact indirectly: `matchesShipped` uses strict `==` comparison, and the refresh tests only pass because the snapshot equals the historical body exactly.

Wrote a throwaway probe test (removed after running) that: (1) placed the d1b4bebf pre-guard ms-spec-grill body via installSkills → refreshed to the guarded canonical (14913 -> 15399 bytes), confirming the auto-refresh mechanism works for a real existing install, not just the pinned unit test. (2) Took the CURRENT canonical ms-spec-grill content and changed one word inside the new headless-guard sentence itself ('into the void' -> 'into the abyss') → installSkills left it byte-for-byte untouched and recorded a 'user-modified' notice. HC-6 holds even for edits localized to the newly-added guard text, not just edits elsewhere in the file.

Direct `diff` confirms plugins/mindspec/skills/ms-spec-grill/SKILL.md == .claude/skills/ms-spec-grill/SKILL.md == .agents/skills/ms-spec-grill/SKILL.md, and .claude/skills/ms-spec-create/SKILL.md == .agents/skills/ms-spec-create/SKILL.md, post-change. grep for 'one question at a time'/'Cardinal rule'/'ms-spec-grill' across bench/ and internal/harness/ (*.go, *.md) found only a historical HISTORY.md session-log paragraph (documentation, not an assertion) — no code or test string-matches the old skill body.

findings.area: approve-gate integration (undiscovered by the fix author, not covered by any new test)
findings.detail: internal/validate/spec.go checkOpenQuestions (line ~244) fires a SevError for any trimmed line starting with '- [ ]' under Open Questions; internal/approve/spec.go ApproveSpec (line ~47) returns an error and does not proceed when vr.HasFailures(). I unit-probed checkOpenQuestions directly with sections={"Open Questions": "- [ ] grill deferred: headless session — run /ms-spec-grill interactively before approval.\n"} and got exactly one issue: severity=ERROR, message='unresolved open question: - [ ] grill deferred: headless session — run /ms-spec-grill interactively before approval.' This means the deferral marker this fix prescribes DOES hard-block `mindspec approve spec` — which is arguably correct behavior (a headless-deferred spec should not sail to approval ungrilled) but it is completely undocumented: none of ms-spec-approve/SKILL.md, ms-spec-autopilot/SKILL.md, or .claude/agents/spec-orchestrator.md mention the marker or how an orchestrator should react to hitting this specific failure. The net effect for any headless flow that chains spec-create through approve in one unattended run is: the original infinite Q&A stall is replaced by a later, equally-blocking, but now UNTESTED and UNDOCUMENTED validate failure.

internal/harness/scenario_spec_lifecycle.go ScenarioSpecToIdle is a fully headless scenario (its Prompt literally states 'This is an automated, non-interactive run: there is NO human available to answer questions... make reasonable default decisions and proceed') that runs idle->spec->plan->implement->idle in one session and asserts `assertCommandSucceeded(t, events, "mindspec", "approve", "spec")` at line 33. HISTORY.md:941 records this scenario's PASS was achieved via a scenario-prompt-level opt-out workaround against the PRE-0uur skill text ('shipped skill unchanged' at the time). Post-0uur, the shipped skill itself now instructs the agent to defer via the Open-Questions marker whenever it judges itself headless — precisely this scenario's stated condition — which per finding above trips the approve-gate hard error. This is the exact regression surface the bead was meant to fix (turn-stall -> now a different late-stage failure), and it is untested by this diff (no new/updated harness assertions, no note in HISTORY.md).

findings.area: bench/grill eval-collision — live-probed, came back clean
findings.detail: bench/grill/run_eval.sh feeds the full ms-spec-grill SKILL.md (now with the Headless guard) into a single-shot `claude -p` call per fixture, appending the literal instruction 'Do not ask questions; this is a non-interactive batch evaluation.' — phrasing that could plausibly satisfy the guard's own trigger language ('a test harness'). I built the exact prompt the script constructs (SKILL.md + the appended eval instructions + fixtures/spec1.md) and piped it through `claude -p --model claude-opus-4-1-20250805` for real. Output was a full, correctly categorized finding set (1x GROUNDING, 2x SEMANTIC, 1x SYNONYM, 1x CONTRADICTION, 2x EXACT_PHRASE, 2x STRUCTURAL) — no deferral marker, no sign the guard fired. This is one sample of the script's 20 (N_RUNS=5 x 4 fixtures) MIN-aggregated runs and used a different (auto-remapped) model than the script's pinned id, so it reduces but does not eliminate the risk; see concrete_changes_required.

No deterministic signal (env var, CLI flag, TTY check) exists anywhere in the codebase for 'headless' — grep across internal/ and the skill files confirms the guard relies purely on the agent's self-judgment from session context ('invoked by an orchestrator, ms-spec-autopilot, or a test harness'). This is consistent with the repo's existing pattern (the harness's own non-interactive declaration is a prompt-level assertion, not a code signal), so it's the architecturally-consistent choice, but it means both over-trigger and under-trigger are governed entirely by prompt-reading quality with no code-level fallback. Not a blocking issue on its own, but compounds the approve-gate finding: an agent that over-triggers the guard in a genuinely interactive session gets silently downgraded to a deferral, and only discovers this later at approve time via an opaque CLI error.
